name: Chelsea
political_explanation: Chelsea's old hooligan firm, the Chelsea Headhunters, had an extensively documented far-right and National Front/BNP connection through the 1970s-90s, covered in academic hooliganism writing. As with Leeds, Millwall, and West Ham, this describes that historic firm, not Chelsea's fans or the club today.

name: Leeds United
political_explanation: Leeds's old hooligan firm, the Leeds Service Crew, had a documented National Front and far-right connection through the 1970s-80s, according to academic writing on football hooliganism. This describes that historic firm specifically, not Leeds's current fans or the club today - the same is true of the other English hooligan-firm entries here (Chelsea, Millwall, West Ham, Burnley). English football is different from continental Europe in this project: it has essentially no current, politically self-identified ultras groups, so most English entries describe historic hooligan firms rather than active groups.

name: Burnley
political_explanation: Burnley's Suicide Squad is tied to a specific, well-known, and much more recent incident than most English entries here: the 2020 'White Lives Matter Burnley' banner flown over Manchester City's stadium in response to players kneeling for Black Lives Matter, organized by hooligans that included a named member of the English Defence League. Because this is a specific, dated, 2020 event rather than something from the 1970s-80s, Burnley is one of the more currently relevant English entries in this dataset.

name: Millwall
political_explanation: Millwall's hooligan firm, the Bushwackers, is long documented as having recruited for the National Front specifically in the 1970s-80s - not just general far-right sympathy, but active political recruitment happening through the firm. That's a more direct connection than some other entries, which is why this is hardcore. As with the other English entries, this is historic, not current.

name: Celtic
political_explanation: Celtic's Green Brigade has an openly socialist and Irish-republican identity, including displaying communist imagery and organizing Palestinian-solidarity actions that have actually drawn fines from UEFA. Getting sanctioned by UEFA for a political display is strong, official evidence, similar to how Verona's group was sanctioned for racism - it's not just a reputation, it's a documented consequence. Green Brigade is Celtic's main, highly visible ultras-style group today.

name: Rangers
political_explanation: Rangers' main fan group, Union Bears, is described in football journalism as pro-monarchy and loyalist, tied to the wider Ulster loyalist/unionist tradition among some Rangers fans historically. It's coded moderate rather than hardcore because no documentation of violence or hate speech specific to Union Bears (beyond ordinary football hooliganism) was found. The Celtic-Rangers rivalry is a case in this dataset where both sides have a documented, opposing political identity - Celtic's left-republicanism against Rangers' loyalist-unionism.

name: St Johnstone
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: St Mirren
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Bayern Munich
political_explanation: Bayern's Schickeria Munchen is well known for sustained anti-fascist activism, especially a long-running campaign honoring Kurt Landauer, the club's Jewish president before World War II who was persecuted by the Nazis. This campaign has been publicly acknowledged by the club itself, not just reported by outside journalists, which is stronger evidence than typical fan-culture reporting. That sustained, multi-decade, club-acknowledged campaign is why this is hardcore.

name: Bayer Leverkusen
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Eintracht Frankfurt
political_explanation: Eintracht Frankfurt's Ultras Frankfurt '97 is widely cited as one of German football's most explicitly anti-racist groups, running ongoing anti-discrimination campaigns rather than just holding a general reputation. That sustained, active campaigning is why this is hardcore - it's part of a wider pattern of openly anti-fascist German ultras groups that also includes St. Pauli, Union Berlin, Bayern, and Dortmund.

name: Borussia Dortmund
political_explanation: Dortmund's main current ultras group, The Unity, has a publicly documented anti-fascist platform and has actually confronted far-right elements within Dortmund's own fanbase, which is stronger than just holding an anti-fascist reputation - they've acted on it. That's why the club is coded hardcore left-wing overall. Separately, a 1980s hooligan firm called Borussenfront was well-documented as far-right, and one of its former leaders later showed up at a 2014 far-right rally in Cologne - but that's clearly historic and separate from Dortmund's current ultras scene, so it doesn't change the club's current rating.

name: SC Freiburg
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: TSG Hoffenheim
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Union Berlin
political_explanation: Union Berlin's Wuhlesyndikat carries on the club's consistent anti-fascist identity, which traces back to East Berlin's working-class and quietly dissident culture under Communist rule - this is documented in mainstream German journalism, not just ultras-specific sources, which adds weight. That deeper historical grounding is why this is hardcore.

name: Werder Bremen
political_explanation: Werder Bremen is a genuinely mixed case worth explaining honestly. The club's main current ultras groups, Infamous Youth and Sherwood Youth, are described as left-leaning in German fan-culture sources, including a documented friendship with St. Pauli's far-left ultras - that's the more visible, central identity, which is why the club is coded left-wing overall. But a separate, named hooligan crew, Endstufe-Crew Bremen, was specifically identified by anti-fascist monitors among the 'Nazihooligans' at a October 2014 far-right rally in Cologne (HoGeSa). Both things are true at once - a left-leaning ultras culture and a documented far-right hooligan crew can exist at the same club.

name: Borussia Mönchengladbach
political_explanation: Gladbach's ultras scene, including Sottocultura Ultras Gladbach, is described as generally right-leaning in German fan-forum discussions. It's coded moderate rather than hardcore specifically because that source is an informal fan forum rather than journalism or an official statement, which is a weaker type of evidence than most other right-wing entries in this dataset rely on. The underlying claim might well be accurate, it just hasn't been backed up by a stronger source yet.

name: 1. FC Köln
political_explanation: Cologne's fan culture, including the Wilde Horde ultras group, is generally described as anti-fascist and tolerant, which fits the city's broader progressive reputation in Germany. It's coded moderate because the sourcing found is more about the general vibe of the fanbase than a specific campaign or action, unlike the clearer hardcore cases (like Bayern's or Dortmund's ultras) where there's a named, ongoing project.

name: Hamburger SV

name: Schalke 04
political_explanation: Two Schalke groups, Ultras Gelsenkirchen and Marler Jungs, are both documented (by the German fan-culture archive Faszination Fankurve, dated May 2, 2015) as having physically blocked a neo-Nazi rally from reaching Gelsenkirchen - and that's confirmed even by the far-right party that organized the rally, which complained about the blockade itself. That's solid, specific evidence, but it's a single documented incident rather than an ongoing campaign, which is why this is moderate rather than hardcore.

name: Hertha BSC
political_explanation: Hertha's organized support has a documented history of far-right hooligan activity, tied to a banned hooligan network (Hertha Froesche) that's been reported on repeatedly in German media. Unlike Dresden, Magdeburg, and Cottbus, Hertha is a West Berlin club, which shows this isn't just an East German phenomenon in German football. It's coded hardcore because of the specific, named 'banned network' finding rather than a vague association.

name: Energie Cottbus
political_explanation: Cottbus's Inferno Cottbus has a long-documented far-right hooligan presence, regularly cited as one of the clearest post-reunification cases in Germany, alongside Dresden and Magdeburg. Cottbus just got promoted into this dataset for the first time, so this finding came from direct research rather than needing to correct an earlier guess, unlike some other clubs in this project.

name: Darmstadt 98
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Dynamo Dresden
political_explanation: Dresden's Ultras Dynamo is one of the most written-about cases of organized far-right hooliganism in German football, tied to the East German neo-Nazi hooligan scene that grew after reunification. Multiple unrelated sources researched for this project - general hooliganism journalism and a specific piece comparing East German clubs' fan politics - independently landed on the same conclusion, which is why this is hardcore with high confidence.

name: 1. FC Magdeburg
political_explanation: Magdeburg's Blue Generation has a documented history of far-right hooligan incidents, reported repeatedly in German media. This fits a wider, well-known pattern: several former East German clubs (also Dresden and Cottbus, both elsewhere in this dataset) have documented far-right hooligan scenes that German journalists and researchers have written about since reunification. That broader, independently-confirmed pattern adds confidence, which is why this is hardcore.

name: VfL Osnabrück

name: FC St. Pauli
political_explanation: St. Pauli's anti-fascist, anti-racist identity isn't just an ultras group's opinion - it's official club policy. The club itself formally bans fascist symbolism. That makes this the single most institutionally solid left-wing finding in the entire dataset, which is why several other clubs' entries reference friendships with St. Pauli specifically (Werder Bremen's ultras groups, for example) when describing their own left-leaning identity.

name: VfL Wolfsburg
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Paris Saint-Germain
political_explanation: PSG's ultras history was genuinely split for years - the far-right-linked Boulogne end versus the left-wing, multicultural Auteuil end - until the club disbanded all its ultras groups in 2010 after violence. The current group, Collectif Ultras Paris (formed 2016), has organized left-leaning solidarity actions, including raising money after the 2020 Beirut port explosion. It's coded moderate rather than hardcore because that's a newer group without the same long track record as some other hardcore-tier entries.

name: RC Lens
political_explanation: Lens's Red Tigers Lens 1994 are described as anti-racist by the same French publication (Les Utopiques), tied to the region's coal-mining, working-class history. As with Saint-Etienne, it's coded moderate because it rests on one dedicated source rather than being confirmed across multiple places.

name: Olympique de Marseille
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Olympique Lyonnais
political_explanation: This one's worth explaining honestly. The ultras-history site Mundo Ultras actually declines to label Lyon's Bad Gones, saying there's no single stable political identity across the group's whole history. But separately, actual news coverage is extensive and consistent over more than a decade: Spanish outlets have called them 'known throughout Europe for its far-right ideology,' described their politics as 'nationalist and ultra-right,' reported police treating them as far-right for security purposes, and covered Lyon as a known far-right hotspot tied to this group. Weighing one archive site's refusal to label them against that much independent news coverage, this stays hardcore - but it's flagged here as a genuinely closer call than most entries.

name: LOSC Lille
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: AS Monaco
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Stade Rennais
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: RC Strasbourg
political_explanation: The French government itself - specifically the Interior Ministry, in an official circular aimed at several ultras groups - accused Strasbourg's Offenders group of being 'a neo-Nazi cell disguised as a supporters' identity.' A government accusation like that is about the strongest kind of evidence in this whole dataset, stronger than journalism or fan-forum sourcing, which is why this is hardcore.

name: Toulouse FC
political_explanation: Toulouse's main ultras group, Indians Tolosa, is actually documented as anti-racist and officially apolitical - they display anti-racist banners and ban French flags, which carry far-right associations in French ultras culture. But the same source (a French outlet called L'Empaille) reports that this hands-off approach has let a separate, named far-right group, Camside Tolosa, operate within the same stand, including hosting visiting neo-Nazi hooligans. So this is coded as a faction, not because Indians Tolosa itself is right-wing, but because a real, named far-right group exists alongside it.

name: Montpellier HSC
political_explanation: Montpellier's current ultras group, Armata Ultras (which took over after the older Butte Paillade 91 became inactive), openly describes itself as anti-fascist and multicultural, according to a French sports-culture analysis (Dialectik Football) that specifically contrasted Armata's clear stance with softer, more evasive 'apolitical' framing used by similar groups elsewhere in the region. A group stating its own politics directly is why this is hardcore.

name: AS Nancy Lorraine

name: Red Star FC
political_explanation: Red Star's fan group, Red Star Fans, is described by French Wikipedia as anti-fascist and left-leaning, which fits the club's own historic identity tied to Paris's 'red suburb' political tradition. Having both the club's own history and its current fan group point the same direction is why this is hardcore. The group has also faced government dissolution threats in 2025, alongside several other French groups researched for this project.

name: AS Saint-Étienne
political_explanation: Saint-Etienne's Green Angels are described as anti-fascist by a French left-wing supporter-culture publication (Les Utopiques), tied to the club's mining-region, working-class roots. It's coded moderate because this rests on one specific source rather than the kind of sustained, widely-confirmed pattern seen in some hardcore cases.

name: FC Metz
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: FC Sochaux-Montbéliard
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Inter Milan
political_explanation: Reporting has tied a specific hardcore faction within Inter's fan base, Boys San, to far-right political figures and, in some cases, neo-fascist-linked hooligan networks. This is coded as a faction because it's about that specific named group, not Inter's whole ultras scene (Curva Nord Milano), which doesn't have a similar reputation as a whole - if anything, Inter's general fanbase is sometimes described as more conservative-bourgeois in a broad social sense than politically far-right in an organized way.

name: AC Milan
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Napoli
political_explanation: Napoli's organized support, including groups called Ultras Napoli 1972 and Fedayn EAM 1979, has a documented history of organizing against anti-Neapolitan racism - discriminatory chants that fans of northern Italian clubs have historically directed at Naples and its fans. That's a specific, real form of activism, which is why this leans left-wing, but it's coded moderate because it's more about identity and anti-discrimination than a stated broader political ideology like Fiorentina's group.

name: AS Roma
political_explanation: Roma's historic ultras group Fedayn was founded in 1972 by Roberto Rulli with openly left-wing and anarchist politics, drawn from working-class Rome neighborhoods - and it was founded specifically as the political opposite of Roma's other historic group, the right-wing 'Boys.' Having the group's own founding story state its politics directly, rather than having that inferred later, is strong evidence, which is why this is hardcore.

name: Juventus
political_explanation: Juventus's Drughi have been tied to repeated, documented racist incidents - specifically monkey-chanting aimed at Black opposing players - across more than one season, not a single bad night. A repeated pattern of documented racist conduct is enough on its own for a hardcore rating here, even without the group issuing any formal political statement about itself.

name: Como
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Atalanta
political_explanation: Atalanta's Wild Boys (tracing back to a 1980s group called Wild Kaos) is documented as right-leaning, with reported ties to the regional right-wing party Lega Lombarda. This is separate from an older, unrelated left-wing group at the same club, Brigate Nerazzurre, from the 1970s. It's coded moderate because the evidence describes a gradual political association over time rather than a fixed, extreme, or violent identity.

name: Bologna
political_explanation: Bologna's Curva Andrea Costa has a documented left-wing, anti-fascist reputation, which fits Emilia-Romagna's history as one of Italy's most left-leaning regions. It's coded moderate rather than hardcore because the sourcing describes a general reputation rather than a specific campaign or named political statement, unlike Fiorentina's group next door.

name: Lazio
political_explanation: Lazio's Irriducibili are one of the most internationally reported far-right ultras groups in all of European football, with a long, well-documented history of fascist and neo-Nazi symbolism, including pro-Mussolini banners and antisemitic chants. This is probably the single most widely known case of explicit far-right ultras identity in football, cited constantly in journalism and academic writing on the subject, well beyond what was specifically looked up for this project.

name: Udinese
political_explanation: Udinese's historic ultras group, Hooligans Teddy Boys, has a documented history of right-wing and racist actions, including a specific, widely-reported antisemitic incident in 1989 aimed at a new signing. Having an actual dated, named incident is stronger evidence than a general reputation, which is why this is hardcore. It's also one of the older documented cases in this whole dataset.

name: Torino
political_explanation: Italian political journalism (Orizzonti Politici) specifically describes Ultras Torino as the more moderate, left-leaning faction within Torino's fan base - and the same article names a separate, opposing far-right splinter group in the same stand, Torino Hooligans. Since the source itself frames this as one faction among others in a divided fanbase, it's coded as a faction rather than a label for Torino's support as a whole.

name: Sassuolo
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Parma
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Cagliari
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Genoa
political_explanation: Genoa's Gradinata Nord has a documented right-wing, nationalist identity, contrasted with city rival Sampdoria's left-leaning fanbase. It's coded moderate rather than hardcore because the documentation of actual violent or hateful incidents tied to this specific group is thinner than for the clearer hardcore cases in Italy, like Lazio or Verona - it's more of a general right-leaning reputation.

name: Lecce
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Fiorentina
political_explanation: Fiorentina's Collettivo Autonomo Viola takes its name from Italy's 'collettivo autonomo' tradition - specific far-left political terminology, not generic ultras branding - and the group explicitly organizes around anti-fascist politics. Naming yourself after a specific political movement is about as direct as evidence gets, which is why this is hardcore.

name: Venezia
political_explanation: Venezia's Curva Sud Venezia has displayed clear anti-fascist banners and messaging. It's coded moderate rather than hardcore because the evidence found is specific banner displays rather than an ongoing, named campaign like some of the hardcore-tier groups elsewhere.

name: Sudtirol
political_explanation: Sudtirol's current ultras group, Gradinata Nord Bolzano, is directly described by Italian Wikipedia's own article as far-right, tied to an ethnic-Italian identity in a region where German speakers are the majority. Having a source flatly state 'far-right' rather than hedge is why this is hardcore.

name: Carrarese
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Empoli
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Sampdoria
political_explanation: Sampdoria's Ultras Tito Cucchiaroni have a left-leaning identity tied to Genoa's working-class port history, contrasted with city rival Genoa's more right-leaning fanbase. It's coded moderate because the evidence is about a general working-class identity rather than the group explicitly naming its own politics.

name: Hellas Verona
political_explanation: Verona's Brigate Gialloblu have repeatedly displayed swastikas and Celtic crosses at matches, and the club has actually been formally sanctioned by football authorities over racist chanting tied to the group. Official sanctions from a governing body are stronger evidence than just journalism describing a reputation, which is why this is hardcore. The group is still active today, not a thing of the past.

name: Cremonese
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Vicenza
record: 27W-8D-3L, 89 pts — Champions Group A, Serie C 2025-26 (promoted)
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Benevento
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Ascoli
record: 23W-8D-5L, 77 pts — Serie C 2025-26, won promotion play-off
political_explanation: Ascoli's Settembre Bianconero is listed among the right-leaning groups in a well-known Italian newspaper mapping of ultras politics (Corriere della Sera). It's coded moderate because that's one general mapping rather than a specific incident, campaign, or the group's own stated politics.

name: Barcelona
political_explanation: Barcelona's old ultras group, Boixos Nois, had a far-right, Catalan-separatist reputation that was well documented in Spanish press before the club banned the group from the Camp Nou in 2003 after repeated violence and racism. It's coded hardcore because this wasn't a soft lean or a rumor - it was serious enough that the club itself formally kicked the group out. This describes that specific historic group, not Barca's current, much more mainstream fanbase, which has no similar reputation. Matchday atmosphere today comes from official fan clubs (penyes) with no political character.

name: Real Madrid
political_explanation: Real Madrid's old ultras group, Ultra Sur, is described by the ultras-history site Mundo Ultras as having a far-right, neofascist reputation with ties to Francoism, and the club formally banned the group from the Bernabeu in 2013-2014 after years of violence and racism. That's a strong basis for hardcore - a club banning its own hardcore fan group over its politics and conduct is about as serious as this gets. This describes that historic group, not the officially sanctioned fan clubs that now lead matchday atmosphere, which don't share that reputation.

name: Villarreal
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Atletico Madrid
political_explanation: Atletico's Frente Atletico is one of the most written-about far-right ultras groups in European football, with a reported peak membership around 2,500. It's coded hardcore not just because of that reputation but because of a specific, documented event: in 1998, members of a section of the group called 'Bastion' killed a rival Real Sociedad fan, Aitor Zabaleta, outside the old stadium. That's a case where an extreme political reputation and a real act of violence are directly tied together, which is about as strong as evidence gets. Frente Atletico is still active today, not a historic footnote.

name: Real Betis
political_explanation: Betis's main ultras group, Supporters Gol Sur, is specifically named as far-right-linked in a Spanish investigation (El Cierre Digital) into far-right street movements' roots in football, and a second, separate source (Mundo Ultras) backs up a similar characterization. Two independent sources landing on the same conclusion is why this is hardcore rather than moderate. The group remains active and central to Betis's matchday atmosphere today.

name: Celta Vigo
political_explanation: Celta's main ultras group, Celtarras, is described as having a left-leaning, Galician-regionalist identity in Spanish ultras-culture sources. It's coded moderate rather than hardcore because the sourcing describes a general regional/left lean rather than the group stating an explicit political program the way, say, the Basque groups do. It's a real lean, just less sharply defined.

name: Getafe
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Rayo Vallecano
political_explanation: Rayo's Bukaneros openly call themselves the 'symbol of the working class' and tie their identity to Vallecas, a historically left-wing, working-class Madrid neighborhood. This isn't just a reputation - they've backed it up with action, including campaigning against signing a player over his reported far-right sympathies. A group that states its politics outright and then acts on them is about as clear-cut as this gets, which is why it's hardcore.

name: Valencia
political_explanation: Valencia's Ultras Yomos are described as right-wing and nationalist across Spanish ultras-culture sources, and on top of that, a former leader of the group has publicly identified himself as far-right, reportedly using Falangist salutes and slogans. That combination - a group with a consistent reputation, plus an actual named person from the group saying so openly - is why this is coded hardcore rather than a softer moderate label. Ultras Yomos remains Valencia's main ultras group today.

name: Real Sociedad
political_explanation: Real Sociedad's Herri Norte Taldea has a left-wing Basque nationalist identity tied to the Basque independence movement - historically even more directly political than the equivalent group at rival Athletic Bilbao. This is a well-established, decades-old identity documented consistently across Spanish ultras-culture sources, not a one-off claim, which is why it's coded hardcore.

name: Espanyol
political_explanation: Espanyol's old ultras group, Brigadas Blanquiazules, is named as far-right in a Spanish investigative piece (El Cierre Digital) into the football roots of far-right street movements, and separately by the ultras-history site Mundo Ultras. The group was officially disbanded in 2010. It's coded hardcore because two independent sources agree on the same characterization. This describes that historic group - today's matchday atmosphere comes from two newer groups, La Curva and Juvenil 1991, which don't carry the same reputation.

name: Athletic Bilbao
political_explanation: Athletic's Herri Norte shares the same left-wing Basque nationalist identity as Real Sociedad's group of the same name, tied to the Basque independence movement. It's reinforced by the club itself: Athletic only signs Basque-raised players (the cantera policy), which bakes Basque identity politics into the club, not just its fans. That combination of a political ultras group plus a matching club policy is why this is hardcore.

name: Sevilla
political_explanation: Sevilla's Biris Norte has a long-documented left-wing reputation and is generally considered one of the first organized ultras groups in Spanish football, founded in 1975. Sources consistently describe it as left-wing, in direct contrast to city rival Real Betis's far-right-documented group. That long, consistent track record is why this is hardcore.

name: Alaves
political_explanation: Alaves's Indar Gorri (Basque for 'Red Force') is part of the same documented left-wing Basque nationalist ('abertzale') scene as the groups at Osasuna, Athletic Bilbao, and Real Sociedad. The name is shared with Osasuna's group and reflects the same political tradition. It's coded hardcore because the 'abertzale' label is specific and repeated across sources, not a loose inference.

name: Elche
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Levante
political_explanation: Levante's Frente Bukanero is documented as left-wing and anti-fascist, explicitly contrasted in sources with city rival Valencia's right-wing Ultras Yomos. Having both sides of a derby described independently and in opposition to each other adds confidence to both findings. Coded hardcore because the anti-fascist identity is stated directly in sourcing, not inferred from something softer like regional identity.

name: Osasuna
political_explanation: Osasuna's Indar Gorri is described specifically as 'abertzale' (Basque/Navarrese left-nationalist) in Spanish ultras-culture sources - the same term used for the Basque-nationalist groups at Athletic Bilbao, Real Sociedad, and Alaves. That specific, repeated word choice across sources is why this is hardcore rather than a vaguer regional-lean label.

name: Racing Santander
political_explanation: Racing's main ultras group, Juventudes Verdiblancas, has had its public image 'associated in different periods with the far right,' according to the ultras-history site Mundo Ultras. It's coded moderate rather than hardcore because that source hedges its wording ('in different periods') rather than giving the flat, direct label used for hardcore-tier groups elsewhere in Spain.

name: Deportivo La Coruna
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Malaga
political_explanation: Malaga's Frente Bokeron is directly described as extreme-right in Spanish ultras-culture sources. It's coded hardcore because that's a specific, stated label from a dedicated source, not a general impression. The group remains Malaga's main and active ultras presence.

name: Castellon
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Las Palmas
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Cordoba
political_explanation: Cordoba's Brigadas Blanquiverdes is a case where two sources disagree: one Spanish ultras site calls the group apolitical, while a more detailed fan forum (which has checked out accurately on dozens of other groups researched for this project) says plainly 'politically they are nationalist' - using 'nationalist' the same way that source uses it for other right-leaning Spanish groups. Given the second source's track record, this leans right-wing, but it's coded moderate rather than hardcore because there's no documented violence or hate speech tied to the group specifically, just a political label.

name: Sporting Gijon
political_explanation: Sporting Gijon's Ultra Boys are directly described as extreme-right in Spanish ultras-culture sources, including a documented history of physical fights that fits that reputation. Worth noting: earlier research for this project had assumed this group leaned left because Asturias has a strong mining/working-class history - that assumption turned out to be wrong once the specific group was actually looked into, which is a good example of why guessing based on a region's general politics isn't reliable.

name: Eibar
political_explanation: Eibar's Eskozia la Brava is described as extreme-left and 'abertzale' (Basque left-nationalist) in Spanish ultras-culture sources - the same specific political current documented at Athletic Bilbao, Real Sociedad, Osasuna, and Alaves. Eibar is in Gipuzkoa, in the Basque Country, so this fits the wider regional pattern. Coded hardcore for the same reason as the other Basque clubs: it's a specific, named characterization, not a guess.

name: Cadiz
political_explanation: Cadiz's Brigada Amarilla is one of Spain's oldest self-identified left-wing ultras groups, and it keeps company with other left-wing Spanish groups (like Rayo's Bukaneros and the Basque groups) through documented friendships and solidarity. A long track record plus a pattern of aligning with other left-wing groups is why this is hardcore.

name: Albacete
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Real Sociedad B
political_explanation: [This is a reserve team; it shares the same fan base and rating as Real Sociedad.] Real Sociedad's Herri Norte Taldea has a left-wing Basque nationalist identity tied to the Basque independence movement - historically even more directly political than the equivalent group at rival Athletic Bilbao. This is a well-established, decades-old identity documented consistently across Spanish ultras-culture sources, not a one-off claim, which is why it's coded hardcore.

name: Real Valladolid
political_explanation: Valladolid's ultras scene actually split into two rival groups in 1996: Ultras Violetas, described as the club's far-right group, and Fossa Garrafoni, an explicitly far-left splinter. This entry describes Ultras Violetas specifically, since it was the larger and more documented of the two. Both groups are now dissolved, so this reflects Valladolid's ultras history rather than an active group today.

name: Mallorca
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: Eldense
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: ADO Den Haag
political_explanation: ADO Den Haag's hardcore support (North Side) has a history of racist-chanting incidents, and that pattern got much stronger evidence recently: a banner from ADO's own stadium was spotted among rioters at a September 2025 far-right riot in The Hague, where people gave Nazi salutes alongside Defend Netherlands, a group formally classified as far-right by Dutch intelligence. That's a documented, recent, specific event on top of an existing pattern, which is why it's hardcore.

name: Ajax
political_explanation: Before the November 2024 Ajax-Maccabi Tel Aviv match, Ajax's F-Side said 'politics and football should be kept separate' - while also saying they'd physically stop a planned pro-Palestinian protest near the stadium. Trying to block one side of a political demonstration is itself a political act, whatever the group says about staying neutral. This is a closer call than most entries here, and it doesn't reach the same bar as groups tied to actual violence or hate speech, which is why it's moderate rather than hardcore.

name: Feyenoord
political_explanation: Feyenoord's hardcore support section, Vak S, has faced scrutiny over far-right-linked hooligan incidents and antisemitic chanting directed at rival Ajax. It's coded moderate rather than hardcore because the reporting found describes specific incidents rather than the group stating an ideology outright, unlike some of the clearer hardcore cases elsewhere in this dataset.

name: SC Cambuur
political_explanation: A Cambuur hooligan firm was specifically named on flags at the same September 2025 far-right riot in The Hague where rioters posed under a 'Dutch Firms United' banner alongside Defend Netherlands, a group Dutch intelligence has formally classified as far-right. This was only found once the research for this project widened from searching club-by-club to searching for the broader far-right hooligan movement itself - a good example of how the right search approach matters. It's hardcore because it's direct participation in an organized far-right event, reported by multiple Dutch news outlets.

name: Jong Ajax
political_explanation: [This is a reserve team; it shares the same fan base and rating as Ajax.] Before the November 2024 Ajax-Maccabi Tel Aviv match, Ajax's F-Side said 'politics and football should be kept separate' - while also saying they'd physically stop a planned pro-Palestinian protest near the stadium. Trying to block one side of a political demonstration is itself a political act, whatever the group says about staying neutral. This is a closer call than most entries here, and it doesn't reach the same bar as groups tied to actual violence or hate speech, which is why it's moderate rather than hardcore.

name: FC Volendam
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: NAC Breda
political_explanation: Like Cambuur, an NAC Breda hooligan firm was specifically named on flags at the September 2025 far-right riot in The Hague, alongside the officially classified far-right group Defend Netherlands. This is hardcore for the same reason: direct, named participation in an organized far-right demonstration, reported by multiple Dutch outlets.

name: Heracles Almelo
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.

name: FC Porto
political_explanation: A Portuguese sports news outlet (JPN/JornalismoPortoNet) has documented a specific far-right faction, 'Divisao Azul,' operating within Porto's main fan group, Super Dragoes - while noting that Super Dragoes itself, as a group, doesn't publicly claim any political position. That's the same pattern found at Inter Milan, Sporting, and Benfica: a real, named far-right group exists inside a bigger fan organization that isn't itself political. Super Dragoes remains Porto's main and active fan group today.

name: Sporting CP
political_explanation: Sporting's older fan group, Juventude Leonina (also called Juve Leo), used to include a specific far-right, neo-Nazi faction called 'Grupo 1143,' led by a figure connected to Hammerskins Portugal - this is documented by Portuguese newspapers and has its own dedicated Wikipedia article. A 2005/2007 police intelligence report separately noted far-right elements within Juve Leo generally. Grupo 1143 itself was reported inactive by 2015, so this describes documented history. It's a faction finding because the evidence is about that specific sub-group, not Juve Leo's stated position as a whole. Sporting's current official fan group, Brigada Ultras Sporting, has no similar finding attached to it.

name: SL Benfica
political_explanation: A member of Benfica's No Name Boys group told an interviewer that swastika flags have been displayed within their section of the stadium - that's real hate symbolism regardless of what the group officially claims about itself. But the same member denied the group as a whole has any formal ideology, and a 2005/2007 Portuguese police intelligence report similarly described far-right elements existing within Benfica's fan scene generally, rather than pinning it on one specific group. So this is coded as a faction: real, documented far-right presence, but bounded to part of the group rather than all of it.

name: Sporting CP B
political_explanation: [This is a reserve team; it shares the same fan base and rating as Sporting CP.] Sporting's older fan group, Juventude Leonina (also called Juve Leo), used to include a specific far-right, neo-Nazi faction called 'Grupo 1143,' led by a figure connected to Hammerskins Portugal - this is documented by Portuguese newspapers and has its own dedicated Wikipedia article. A 2005/2007 police intelligence report separately noted far-right elements within Juve Leo generally. Grupo 1143 itself was reported inactive by 2015, so this describes documented history. It's a faction finding because the evidence is about that specific sub-group, not Juve Leo's stated position as a whole. Sporting's current official fan group, Brigada Ultras Sporting, has no similar finding attached to it.

name: Torreense
political_explanation: No supporter group with a documented political position was identified for this club in the research conducted for this project.
